student answer:
Q: Describe how one function of HIS revenue cycle management functionality increases a provider''s revenue. A:
  • Electronic eligibility verification built into the HIS revenue cycle system helps providers increase their revenue by confirming patient insurance coverage before any services are delivered1. When the front desk staff registers a patient, the system automatically checks whether that person''s insurance is currently active and what specific services their plan will actually pay for3. This real-time verification means the provider discovers coverage problems at registration rather than weeks later when submitting claims1.
  • By catching these issues early, the provider avoids performing services that would never generate any payment because the insurance had lapsed or didn''t cover that particular treatment. Without this automated checking capability, providers often deliver care assuming the patient has valid coverage, only to have claims denied later and discover they cannot collect payment for work already completed3. The eligibility verification function essentially acts as a gatekeeper that protects revenue by ensuring the provider only moves forward with services they can reasonably expect to get paid for1.
  • This function also reduces the administrative costs associated with claim denials and resubmissions3. When claims get rejected due to eligibility issues, staff must spend time researching the problem, contacting the patient about alternative payment arrangements, and potentially writing off the charges as bad debt1. By preventing these scenarios upfront, the eligibility verification system preserves revenue that would otherwise be lost to uncollectable accounts3.
  • This automated functionality transforms what used to be a manual, error-prone process into a systematic safeguard that directly protects the provider''s financial performance by ensuring payment feasibility before services are rendered1.

Sources:
  1. CS581 Class Notes 09-29-25 Rev Cycle, Pop Health.pdf
  2. https://claude.ai
  3. Houser, S. Health IT and EHRs: Principles and Practice, 2024 (7th ed.). American Health Information Management Association (AHIMA). https://reader2.yuzu.com/books/978-1-58426-965-6
